stimulus: Education researcher Yara Oduya hypothesizes that, when teaching unfamiliar policy issues to high school students, a collaborative group discussion in which several students with different viewpoints share ideas in a supportive setting is more informative for an observing audience than a head-to-head debate between two students arguing opposing positions is. To test Oduya's hypothesis, a team of graduate students recruited audience members and randomly assigned each to watch one of three classroom segments on the same unfamiliar issue: (1) a single student giving a brief presentation, (2) a two-student debate, or (3) a four-student collaborative discussion with varied perspectives.
stem: Which finding from the team's study, if true, would most strongly support Oduya's hypothesis?

answerOptions.content: On average, audience members rated the debaters as more knowledgeable about the issue than the discussion participants.
On average, audience members rated the discussion participants as more knowledgeable about the issue than the single presenter.
On average, audience members who watched the collaborative discussion answered more factual questions about the issue correctly than those who watched the debate or the single presenter did.
On average, audience members who watched the single presenter answered more factual questions about the issue correctly than those who watched the debate did.

rationale:
Choice C is correct. Oduya's hypothesis is that a collaborative group discussion (multiple viewpoints, supportive setting) is more informative than a head-to-head debate. Showing that the discussion audience answered more factual questions correctly than the debate audience (and the single-presenter audience) directly demonstrates that the discussion format was the most informative one—exactly what the hypothesis predicts.

Choice A is incorrect: perceptions of how knowledgeable the debaters seemed do not show whether the audience actually learned more. Choice B is incorrect: a perception comparison between two formats (and one that the hypothesis isn't even framed around) doesn't establish that the collaborative discussion is more informative than the debate. Choice D is incorrect: comparing the single presenter to the debate is irrelevant; the hypothesis concerns the collaborative discussion versus the debate.
